summary: Cathy O'Neil responds to a female composer's essay about thriving as a woman in a male-dominated field, arguing that such success stories create survivorship bias. While acknowledging that being distinctive can sometimes help, she points out that we rarely hear from women who were 'squeezed out' of these fields. She reflects on her own experience of adopting gender-neutral presentation in grad school to be seen as a mathematician rather than a woman, suggesting that successful women in male fields may share coping mechanisms that allow them to minimize their perceived otherness.
key_points: Success stories from women in male-dominated fields create survivorship bias - we don't hear from those who left
Women who survive in male environments may have developed coping mechanisms to ignore their own otherness
The author wore gender-neutral clothing in grad school to be seen primarily as a mathematician, not as feminine
Celebrating being a 'woman in a man's field' may mask the contortions women make to succeed
The attention women receive in male fields isn't surprising or undue - it's something survivors train themselves to ignore
money_quotes: We hear way more from the people who stay and 'succeed', which tends to give us massive survivorship bias.
I think we'd find that the women who survive in nearly all-male environments have developed, or were born with, coping mechanisms which allow them to ignore their own otherness.
I didn't want to be seen as primarily feminine. Instead I wanted to be seen as primarily a mathematician.
We still seem to be contorting ourselves in an effort to not seem too womanly, and this makes me think it's entirely un-coincidental, and possibly a crucial part of what allows us to succeed.
I don't think it's undue attention at all – I think it's just something we train ourselves not to consider because focusing on it too much could be paralyzing.
key_insight: The women who succeed in male-dominated fields may share a common trait of minimizing their own otherness, which creates survivorship bias when they later celebrate being women in those spaces.
